title: Wood Closet Shelving
summary: Freestanding ladder-leg closet shelving from 1x4s and 2x4s — slatted shelves thread through leg supports, sized to any closet, with wood or iron-pipe rods.
description:
This replaces the sagging wire shelf with a system that looks like furniture and is built like scaffolding: ladder-style leg supports (two 1x4 legs sandwiching 2x4 shelf rungs) stood along the closet, with 1x4 shelf boards run across them the full length of the closet. Four 1x4s side by side make each shelf — slatted, so the closet breathes and you can see what's on the shelf above your head.

Because the shelf boards are cut to YOUR closet's length, the cut list here covers the leg supports (the repeating unit — build one per 3 feet of closet, minimum two) and leaves the shelf lengths to your tape measure. The assembly sequence in step 2 is the part worth reading twice: outer leg supports first, tied together by the outer shelf boards, then the middle legs positioned with a level — and, if you're using them, located by the closet rods themselves, which is a self-jigging trick that guarantees the rods end up level and the legs end up where the rods need them.

The source finishes with a genuinely useful cost fork: iron-pipe closet rods look fantastic and cost a small fortune; the same function comes from 2x4 blocking stood tall wherever a rod goes, with standard rod sockets and a wood dowel between. Pick by budget, not by function — they hold clothes identically.

Stain the boards BEFORE assembly (the source did — dried overnight, touched up cut ends after). Staining assembled slatted shelving means staining forty slots with a brush you'll come to hate.

tools.note: Pilot holes near 1x4 ends — they split without them.
Positions the middle leg supports. Borrowed from the closet-rod trick in step 2.

materials.name: 1x4, 8 ft
materials.note: Two per leg support plus four per full-length shelf — quantity shown suits an 8-ft closet with two full shelves and two short ones. Recount for yours.

materials.name: 2x4, 8 ft
materials.note: Shelf rungs, rod blocking, and one spare for cutting around knots — the source's advice, and correct.

materials.name: Self-tapping screws, 2-1/2"
materials.note: Legs to 2x4 rungs, two per joint.

materials.name: Self-tapping screws, 1-1/2"
materials.note: Shelf boards to rungs. Brad nails work but screws hold up to reloading the closet forever.

materials.name: Closet rod sockets, pair
materials.note: With wood dowel rods — the budget fork. Iron pipe flanges and pipe are the pricey, pretty fork.

materials.name: Wood dowel, 1-1/4", 6 ft
materials.note: Cut to fit between rod supports.

materials.name: Stain or paint
materials.note: Applied to the boards BEFORE assembly. Touch up cut ends after.

cutList.note: Two per leg support; quantities here are for two supports — add two legs per extra support. Shorten if they won't clear your doorway on the way in.
Three per leg support. Using iron-pipe rods? Measure the pipe assembly FIRST and confirm 15-1/2" depth clears it.
Stood TALL (on edge), not flat, wherever a rod hangs — carries the rod sockets.

steps.title: Prefinish the lumber
steps.body: Stain or paint every board before you cut and build, and let it dry overnight. A slatted shelf system has dozens of inside faces and slots that are miserable to finish after assembly and trivial to finish as flat boards on sawhorses. You'll touch up the cut ends at the end.

steps.title: Build the leg supports
steps.body:
Each support is a ladder: two 81-1/2" 1x4 legs with three 15-1/2" 2x4 rungs between them, glued and screwed with the 2-1/2" screws, two per joint. Build one support per 3 feet of closet, two minimum.

Pilot-drill the 1x4s — screws near the end of 3/4" stock split it. If you're using iron-pipe rods, mount them to the supports now, while everything lies flat; installing pipe flanges inside a standing closet is knuckle work.

steps.title: Set the outer supports and thread the shelves
steps.body:
Cut the shelf boards to your closet's length. Stand the two OUTER leg supports, thread two shelf boards through at the bottom shelf and two near the top, and screw them to the legs, flush to the legs' inside faces — that ties the ends of the system together and makes it self-standing.

Now place the middle supports: use a level, and if rods are involved, let the rod itself dictate where the middle legs stand — hang it in its sockets and slide the leg until the rod is level and seated. Then fill in the remaining shelf boards, outer boards first, middle two spaced evenly, 1-1/2" screws throughout.

Last: screw the unit to the wall studs. It's stable freestanding, but a closet gets yanked on — anti-tip screws are two minutes now versus a very bad surprise later.

steps.title: Add short shelves where they fit
steps.body: Measure between any two leg supports, cut extra 2x4 rungs and 1x4 boards to that span, and drop in as many partial shelves as the closet's contents want — shoe height, purse height, whatever. This is the step where the system earns the word 'custom.'

steps.title: Rods on a budget
steps.body:
Where you want a hanging rod without iron-pipe prices: add a 15-1/2" 2x4 stood TALL (on edge) at each end of the rod run, screw rod sockets to the blocking, and cut a wood dowel to fit between them. Same function as the pipe, small fraction of the cost, and the sockets are replaceable if a dowel ever wears.

Touch up stain on all the cut ends, load the closet, and enjoy shelves that don't bow under winter sweaters.

images.alt: Closet fitted with wooden ladder-leg shelving, slatted shelves, and hanging rods
